subject: URGENT: Wire Transfer Authorization Needed
body:
Hi there,

I'm currently in back-to-back meetings and need your help with an urgent wire transfer for a confidential acquisition.

I need you to process a wire transfer of $47,500 to our vendor immediately. This is time-sensitive as the deal closes today.

Here are the wire instructions:

Bank: First International Bank
Account: 884739201456
Routing: 021000021
Beneficiary: Global Ventures LLC
Reference: CONFIDENTIAL-ACQ-2024

Please confirm once processed. Do not discuss this with anyone else as it's a confidential matter.

Thanks,
Michael Chen
Chief Financial Officer

Sent from my iPhone

red_flags.description: CFO's email uses suspicious 'company-secure.net' domain instead of company domain
Creates urgency and asks to keep secret - classic CEO fraud tactics
Uses 'Hi there' instead of addressing by name
Unusual wire transfer request via email, asking to bypass normal procedures

explanation: This is a CEO fraud (BEC) phishing email. The attacker impersonates the CFO using a lookalike domain. The urgency, secrecy, and unusual wire request are red flags. Always verify wire transfers through a separate communication channel.

subject: Your password expires in 3 days
body:
TechCorp IT Department

Hello John Smith,

Your TechCorp network password will expire in 3 days (January 17, 2024).

To avoid any disruption to your access, please update your password before the expiration date.

How to change your password:

  1. Log in to the IT Self-Service Portal
  2. Click 'Change Password'
  3. Follow the password requirements

Password Requirements:

  • Minimum 12 characters
  • Include uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols
  • Cannot reuse previous 5 passwords

If you need assistance, please contact the IT Help Desk at ext. 4357 or it-help@techcorp.com.

Best regards,
TechCorp IT Support Team

TechCorp Internal Systems | Do not forward this email
explanation: This is a legitimate IT notification. The sender domain matches the company domain (techcorp.com), the link goes to an internal portal, and it includes specific employee information. The tone is professional and provides proper contact channels.

subject: Your mailbox storage is full - Immediate action required
body:
Microsoft 365

Dear User,

red_flags.description: Uses 'Dear User' instead of your name
Creates false urgency with account suspension threat

explanation: This is a phishing email. Microsoft never sends storage warnings via email with upgrade links. Official Microsoft emails come from 'microsoft.com' domains only. The suspicious link is designed to steal your Microsoft credentials.

subject: Important: Benefits Enrollment Period Opens Today
body:
TechCorp Human Resources

Dear TechCorp Employees,

The annual benefits enrollment period begins today, January 12, 2024, and runs through January 26, 2024.

This is your opportunity to:

  • Review and update your health insurance coverage
  • Enroll in or adjust your 401(k) contributions
  • Update dependent information
  • Review voluntary benefits (dental, vision, life insurance)
explanation: This is a legitimate HR benefits enrollment email. The sender domain matches the company (techcorp.com), the link goes to an internal benefits portal, and it includes specific dates and contact information. The tone is informative without creating false urgency.

subject: You have 3 new messages on LinkedIn
body:
LinkedIn

Hi John,

You have 3 unread messages waiting for you on LinkedIn.

SM

Sarah Miller

Hi John, I came across your profile and...

DR

David Rodriguez

Thanks for connecting! I'd love to...

LJ

Lisa Johnson

Are you open to new opportunities? I...

VIEW MESSAGES

Don't miss out on these professional opportunities. Messages expire after 30 days.

LinkedIn Corporation | 1000 W Maude Ave, Sunnyvale, CA 94085

red_flags.description: LinkedIn messages don't expire - this is a fake urgency tactic

explanation: This is a phishing email. LinkedIn only sends emails from 'linkedin.com' domains. The suspicious link is designed to steal your LinkedIn credentials. LinkedIn messages also don't expire, so that claim is false.
